content:
How I Built and Launched 3 Digital Products as a Nigerian Creator With No Audience
A real, unfiltered account of starting from zero — no big following, no investor money, just a phone and a decision to start.*
---
Two weeks ago, I had zero digital products, 370 Instagram followers, and no idea if anyone would ever pay me for anything I created.
Today, I have three products live across four platforms, a free resource that's reached hundreds of people, and — if I'm being completely honest — still no sales.
My name is Aniekan. I'm writing this not because I've "made it," but because I think the messy, in-progress version of this story is more useful than the polished highlight reel everyone else posts after they've already succeeded.
The Problem I Kept Running Into
I'm based in Port Harcourt, Nigeria, and like a lot of people around me, I'd watched countless videos about "making money online," tried a few things half-heartedly, and never actually finished building anything.
The advice I kept hearing was the same: build an audience first, then sell to them. But that felt backwards to me. I didn't have an audience. I didn't have years to wait for one. What I did have was a phone, some knowledge I'd picked up about AI tools, and a stubborn refusal to wait for "the right time."
So I flipped the order. I decided to build the product first, and figure out the audience as I went.
What I Actually Built
**The first product** was the most ambitious — a complete toolkit covering AI tools, WhatsApp sales scripts, and business frameworks specifically for African creators and freelancers. I priced it at ₦10,000 (about $17), anchored against a "regular price" of ₦25,000 to make the value clear.
**The second product** came from a simple realization: ₦10,000 might be too big a first step for someone who's never bought anything from me before. So I built a smaller, ₦2,000 guide — "How To Make Your First $100 Online As An African Creator" — designed purely as an easy entry point. Low risk, low price, real value.
**The third product** is still sitting unpublished, actually. I built a 90-day content system but decided to hold off launching it until I had more proof that my first two products were actually resonating. There's no point building a third rung on a ladder nobody's climbing yet.
Setting Up the Infrastructure (The Unglamorous Part)
Nobody talks about this part, but it took real time: connecting Selar for Nigerian buyers, Gumroad for international ones, setting up Paystack as a direct payment option, verifying a Payoneer account to receive USD, and building a simple sales page using a free hosting tool.
Each of these had its own small battles — payment gateways that needed specific bank account types, sharing permissions on Google Drive that defaulted to "restricted" instead of "public," API keys that needed to be copied exactly right. None of it was hard, exactly, but all of it took patience.
What Actually Happened After Launch
Here's the part most success stories skip: I sent broadcasts to over 500 WhatsApp contacts. I posted in eight Facebook groups. I created Pinterest pins, optimized my Instagram bio, started a TikTok account, and began posting a daily quote series to build consistency.
And for the first week and a half — nothing. No sales. Some views, a few likes, but no actual purchases.
I won't pretend that didn't sting a little. But here's what I noticed instead of panicking:
A stranger on Gumroad from the United States viewed my product page — proof that international reach, however small, was real. Someone in Nigeria got all the way to the checkout page on Selar before abandoning the transaction — proof that real purchase intent existed, even if it didn't convert that particular time. A friend I gave free access to, in exchange for honest feedback, is currently going through it and will tell me, brutally honestly, what's working and what isn't.
None of these are sales. But they're data. And data, even when it's not a "yes," tells you something useful.
## What I'm Learning As I Go
**Free value builds trust faster than any sales pitch.** I created a free pack of 20 AI prompts and gave it away with zero strings attached. It led to a genuine WhatsApp conversation, then an actual in-person meeting with someone who wanted help understanding how to use it. He didn't buy anything that day. But he left with real value, and I left with the quiet confidence that what I'm building actually helps people — even when it doesn't immediately convert to money.
**Cold traffic and warm traffic behave completely differently.** Pinterest sends people who are curious but rarely ready to buy immediately. WhatsApp sends people who already know me, which means conversations move faster, even if they still take time to become sales.
**Consistency feels pointless until it isn't.** Posting a quote every single day for over a week, with zero comments most days, requires a strange kind of stubborn faith. I don't fully know yet if it's "working." But I know that stopping wouldn't have moved me any closer to finding out.
Where This Stands Right Now
I haven't made a sale yet. I want to be honest about that, because I think there's real value in showing this stage of the process, not just the eventual win.
What I have built is real infrastructure, real content, real conversations, and real proof that interest exists, even if conversion hasn't followed yet. I'm choosing to trust that the same patience that got me through tax forms and bank verifications will get me through this part too.
If you're an African creator, freelancer, or small business owner who's been circling the idea of building your first digital product — I'd genuinely love to hear where you are in that process. And if you want to see exactly what I built, you can find both the toolkit and the beginner guide here: aniekanokpodigital.tiiny.site
---
I write about building digital products and AI tools as a Nigerian creator, one honest update at a time. If this resonated, follow along for what happens next — including whether that abandoned Selar transaction ever comes back.
